title: Today's AI Brief: Microsoft to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build
summary: Microsoft's Build conference is set to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements, including a Copilot super app and a new reasoning AI model.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Microsoft to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build
imageCaption: Microsoft to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I've got my eyes on the latest news from the world of artificial intelligence. Microsoft's annual Build conference is just around the corner, and it's shaping up to be a big deal for AI enthusiasts.

First up, Microsoft is set to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build. According to a report from The Verge, the conference will include a Copilot super app, a new reasoning AI model, and plenty of Windows improvements. My read is that this is a big move for Microsoft, as they're looking to integrate AI more deeply into their products.

Next, I'm looking at a story from IEEE Spectrum about a new server that's hoping to break through AI's 'memory wall'. The idea is that current AI models are limited by their memory, and this new server is designed to help overcome that constraint. It's a fascinating problem, and I'm curious to see how it plays out.

Also on the board is the ongoing challenge of data boundaries in AI. As models get bigger and more complex, they require more and more data to train. It's a classic chicken-and-egg problem – do we need more data to train better models, or do we need better models to make use of the data we have?

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
